sound_type: winnowing -- eerie tremolo produced mechanically by outer tail feathers vibrating during steep display dives; NOT vocal; ~25 Hz oscillation; 5th mechanical sound in engine; E=4
ecosystem: Wet meadows, marshes, boggy fields, freshwater wetlands with soft muddy substrate. Cryptic in marshy vegetation. Nearly invisible on the ground.
season: Summer resident/breeder in PNW. Winnowing display spring and early summer (April-June). Crepuscular -- dawn and dusk.

coordinates.description: Wilson's Snipe in wet meadows and marshes of the PNW lowlands and east-side valleys. Winnowing display over breeding habitat.

taxonomy_note.description: FIRST SCOLOPACIDAE entry. Sandpiper family enters the engine. 5th mechanical sound. Winnowing produced by tail feather vibration during display dives. Origin of 'snipe hunt' metaphor. Nearly invisible cryptic bird.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Gallinago from Latin 'gallina' (hen) -- hen-like bird. delicata from Latin 'delicatus' (delicate/fine). Wilson's: honoring Alexander Wilson, father of American ornithology.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Gallinago -- snipes. ~18 species worldwide. Long-billed, cryptic, marsh-dwelling. Many species produce winnowing displays.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Charadriiformes. Fourth Charadriiformes family in sequence (Haematopodidae 166, Charadriidae 167, Scolopacidae 168).
taxonomy_note.family_position: First Scolopacidae. Entered through mechanical sound. 98 species worldwide. Largest shorebird family.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Winnowing: oscillation at ~25 Hz. Tonal frequency per pulse: 300-600 Hz. Low, resonant, vibrating.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 300-500 Hz per pulse. The low, thrumming quality of the winnowing.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: ~25 Hz oscillation rate. 300-600 Hz tonal per pulse.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Per dive: 1-2 seconds. Display session: 10-30+ minutes of repeated circuit-dive-winnow cycles.
song_metrics.note_structure: Winnowing: pulsating tremolo 'whoo-whoo-whoo-whoo' produced during steep dive. ~25 Hz oscillation. Eerie, wavering. Scaip flush call: sharp nasal burst.
song_metrics.note_types: Winnowing (primary -- mechanical, display), scaip (flush call -- vocal, sharp), chip-a (perched call -- vocal, repeated).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Winnowing: 1-2 second tremolo per dive, repeated in circuit-dive-winnow pattern over 10-30+ minutes. Not vocal phrasing -- aerodynamic event.
song_metrics.timbre: Eerie, ghostly, pulsating, otherworldly. Unlike any vocal bird sound. The mechanical origin creates a unique timbral quality -- vibrating feather rather than vibrating membrane.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Moderate volume but remarkable atmospheric presence. 200-500m in calm twilight. Pervasive -- seems to fill the sky from everywhere.
song_metrics.tempo: ~25 Hz oscillation within each winnowing dive. Dive repetitions every 30-60 seconds during display circuit.
song_metrics.context: Territorial display. Breeding season. Crepuscular timing (dawn/dusk). Male advertising territory and attracting female.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. Mechanical sound determined by feather structure, which is consistent.
song_metrics.key_property: WINNOWING -- 5th mechanical sound, tail feather vibration, ~25 Hz tremolo, eerie, crepuscular. FIRST SCOLOPACIDAE. 'Snipe hunt' origin. Nearly invisible. E=4. v1.49.308.

plumage.overall: Cryptic brown striped pattern matching dead marsh vegetation. Bold head stripes (dark crown with pale central stripe, dark eye-line). Long, straight bill. Short legs. Stocky body.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Cryptic brown stripes. Very long straight bill. Bold head pattern. On wet ground in marshy habitat. Nearly invisible until flushed.
plumage.flight_pattern: Explosive zigzag flush from ground. Sharp 'scaip!' call. During display: large high circles with repeated steep dives (winnowing).

physical_description.overall: Length: 25-28 cm. Weight: 80-140g. Bill: 6-7 cm, straight, flexible tip with sensory nerve endings for detecting prey in mud.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Long straight bill. Cryptic stripes. Explosive flush. Winnowing display.
physical_description.field_id: Nearly invisible in marsh until flushed -- then explosive zigzag flight with sharp 'scaip!' In spring twilight, listen for eerie pulsating winnowing from high overhead.

nodes.title: Winnowing -- 5th Mechanical Sound
nodes.description: Eerie tremolo produced by outer tail feathers (rectrices 7-8) vibrating during steep display dives. ~25 Hz oscillation. 1-2 seconds per dive. NOT vocal -- proven by wind tunnel experiments. Body in flight is the instrument.

nodes.title: Tail Feather Mechanics
nodes.description: Rectrices 7 and 8 have stiff, narrow vanes. Extended into airstream during 45-degree dive from 60-100m. Aerodynamic flutter at ~25 Hz. Wind tunnel confirmation: feathers alone produce the winnowing.

nodes.title: Crepuscular Display Flights
nodes.description: Dawn and dusk. Large circuits at 60-100m altitude. Repeated dive-winnow-climb cycles. 10-30+ minute sessions. Spring/early summer. Territorial and mate-attraction function.

nodes.title: 'Snipe Hunt' -- The Invisible Bird
nodes.description: Real bird, nearly invisible. Cryptic plumage matching marsh vegetation. Freezes until stepped on, then explosive zigzag flush. Winnowing from unseen source at twilight. The invisible bird became metaphor for fool's errand.

nodes.title: First Scolopacidae -- Sandpiper Family Enters
nodes.description: Scolopacidae enters at degree 168. 98 species worldwide. Largest shorebird family. Entered through mechanical sound (winnowing) rather than typical shorebird ecology. Fourth Charadriiformes family.

cross_references.relationship: CHARADRIIFORMES! Plover (167) to sandpiper (168). Vocal alarm to mechanical winnowing. Open ground to wet meadow. Both shorebirds.
MECHANICAL SOUNDS! Grouse: wing beats (percussion). Snipe: tail feather vibration (tremolo). Both non-vocal. Both spring display. Forest vs meadow.
CRYPTIC COMPARISON! Both nearly invisible. Mountain Quail in dense brush. Snipe in marsh vegetation. Both detected primarily by sound.
DISPLAY DIVE! Both produce mechanical sounds during display dives. Nighthawk: boom. Snipe: winnowing. Both crepuscular.
